items.headline: Heads up: Claude's priciest model, Fable 5, stops being free-with-your-plan today
items.summary: Anthropic's flagship model Fable 5 came back online July 1 with a one-week grace period where Pro, Max, and Team plans got it included at no extra cost. That grace period ends today, July 7. Starting July 8, using Fable 5 requires turning on prepaid "usage credits" and paying API rates of $10 per million input tokens and $50 per million output tokens, making it the single most expensive model Anthropic sells.
items.why_it_matters: If you've been using Fable 5 inside Claude.ai, Claude Code, or Cowork, go turn on usage credits in Settings today or it will simply stop responding once your free weekly allowance runs out, mid-session, with no warning. If you've never touched Fable 5, nothing changes for your regular Claude usage.

opportunity.action_note: Log into claude.ai Settings, then Usage, and turn on usage credits today if you use Fable 5, so it doesn't cut out mid-project once your free weekly allowance runs out tomorrow.
opportunity.angle: Quick PSA for anyone on Claude: check your settings before Fable 5 starts costing you extra

items.headline: Claude Code now pauses to ask permission before every action, by default
items.summary: Anthropic changed Claude Code's default setting so it stops and waits for your approval before running commands, across the command-line tool, VS Code, and JetBrains plugins. Previously it could keep working through a task without checking in as often.
items.why_it_matters: If you or someone helping you has set up Claude Code to run tasks unattended, like batch-renaming files or running a script overnight, this change means it may now sit there waiting for a click instead of finishing on its own, so it's worth a quick check on anything you've automated.

items.headline: Claude Code's weekly usage limit is 50% higher right now, but only through July 13
items.summary: Since May, Anthropic has been giving Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise subscribers 50% more weekly usage on Claude Code as it brought new computing capacity online. That temporary boost is scheduled to end July 13, after which weekly limits drop back to normal.
items.why_it_matters: If you lean on Claude Code for editing scripts, automating small business tasks, or building things for your channel, this week is a good time to knock out anything bigger before your weekly cap shrinks back down.

items.headline: Google's Gemini can now generate images of you using your Gmail, Photos, and YouTube history, free
items.summary: Google removed the paywall on Gemini's "personalized" image generation, so any eligible US user 13 or older can now try it for free, not just paid subscribers. Gemini uses data from apps you've connected, like Gmail, Google Photos, YouTube, and Search, to generate images that reflect your actual interests and even pull real photos of you, without you uploading anything.
items.why_it_matters: This could be a fast way to whip up personalized graphics, but it only works because it's reading your connected Google accounts, so it's worth a quick look at what you've connected before you try it, especially if you use Gmail or Photos for business.

items.headline: Security researchers found the first ransomware attack run entirely by an AI agent, no hacker at the keyboard
items.summary: Cybersecurity firm Sysdig documented an attack it calls JADEPUFFER, where an AI agent broke into an outdated, internet-exposed piece of server software on its own, stole passwords, moved to a company's real database, encrypted it, and deleted the originals, adapting to problems along the way like a human attacker would, without anyone directing each step.
items.why_it_matters: If you or anyone helping you runs your own website, server, or self-hosted tool, this is a reminder that AI now makes this kind of attack cheap to pull off, so keeping software updated and passwords changed from their defaults matters more than ever.

opportunity.angle: AI just ran an entire ransomware attack by itself, here's what small business owners should actually do about it

categories.title: Tools & Tips for Creators and Small Biz

items.headline: OpusClip's new Android app turns your long videos into short clips right from your phone
items.summary: OpusClip, the AI tool that automatically pulls short highlight clips out of long-form video, launched an Android app on July 2. You can paste a YouTube link or upload a video from your phone, set a clip length and aspect ratio, and get short clips back scored on how likely they are to catch on.
items.why_it_matters: If you've been sitting down at your desktop just to cut a livestream or podcast into a few short clips, this means you can knock that out from your phone between errands, no computer required.

items.headline: YouTube expands its brand-deal matchmaking tool, plus a free crash course on landing sponsorships
items.summary: YouTube's Creator Partnerships program, which matches eligible creators with brands directly inside YouTube Studio, expanded to the UK, Germany, Japan, and Singapore on July 1. Alongside it, YouTube released "The Brand Deal Desk," a free 5-episode video series covering how to pitch, negotiate, and report results to sponsors.
items.why_it_matters: If landing a sponsor has felt like guesswork, this gives you a legitimate way to get matched with brands plus a plain, step-by-step guide to the whole process, worth watching even if Creator Partnerships isn't available in your area yet.

items.headline: YouTube Studio can now search your comments by meaning, not just exact words
items.summary: YouTube rolled out new AI comment tools inside Studio on June 26: a search that finds comments about a topic even when people don't use your exact search words, a panel that surfaces common comment themes on its own, and a "find similar comments" option. It's live on desktop for creators everywhere.
items.why_it_matters: Instead of scrolling through hundreds of comments to see what people actually think about, say, your new intro or a product you mentioned, you can now just describe what you're looking for and let YouTube dig it up for you.
